text: The surgeon carefully preserves the hypogastric nerve and the pelvic plexus, maintaining their integrity as delicate membrane-like structures during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the lymph nodes along the internal iliac vessels, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues using robotic assistance.
The surgeon resects lymph nodes along the periosteum of the sacrum, using a soft coagulation system to achieve hemostasis and prevent bleeding from small blood vessels.
The surgeon continues the dissection of lymph nodes along the internal iliac vessels, extending the dissection to the periphery of these vessels.
The surgeon observes lymph node metastasis in the distal portion of the internal iliac vessels during the dissection.
The surgeon observes the pelvic plexus, noting that there is no visible invasion into this structure.
The surgeon removes the lymph nodes along the external iliac vessels, continuing the dissection process in the pelvic region.
The surgeon continues the dissection, advancing towards the anterior aspect of the psoas muscle, following the resection of lymph nodes along the sacrum.

The surgeon removes lymph nodes along the internal obturator muscle, levator ani muscle fascia, and lumbosacral nerve, using a soft coagulation system to clot small blood vessels communicating between the lymph nodes and internal obturator muscles.
The surgeon detaches the lymph nodes from the vesico-hypogastric fascia, carefully separating them from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon detaches lymph nodes caudally along the vesico-gastric fascia, revealing the bladder wall and visualizing the bladder vessels.
The surgeon follows the pubic vein as it flows through the obturator foramen to the external iliac vein, leading to the identification of the obturator nerve.
The surgeon separates the obturator artery and vein, clips them, and then cuts the vessels.
The surgeon completes the removal of lymph nodes on the distal side, ensuring all nodes are detached from surrounding tissues and structures.
The surgeon begins working on the proximal side, focusing on the area where the lymph nodes are detached from the vesico-hypogastric fascia.
The surgeon identifies the obturator nerve at the bifurcation of the external and internal iliac veins, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the obturator artery branching from the internal iliac artery and proceeds to cut it.
The surgeon identifies the internal iliac artery and vein, which are involved with metastatic lymph nodes, highlighting their proximity to the affected tissue.
The surgeon cuts the internal iliac artery, which is visible within the surgical field.

The surgeon separates the internal iliac vein along the surface layer of the sacral nerve, ensuring it is safely secured due to the sparse nature of this tissue layer, which allows for easy detachment.
The surgeon removes the lymph node located along the sacral nerve, following previous identification and dissection of surrounding vascular structures.
The surgeon uses a soft coagulation system to facilitate clotting of small blood vessels, minimizing heat damage to the nearby nerve structures.
The surgeon cuts the peripheral vessels prior to addressing the internal iliac vein, ensuring clear access to the vein.
The surgeon clips multiple inferior vesicle vessels individually before cutting them, ensuring precise control over vessel management.
The surgeon divides the lymph nodes from the bladder wall, carefully separating these structures from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon clips and cuts the proximal side of the internal iliac vein, completing the division of this vascular structure.
The surgeon removes metastatic lymph nodes located along the sacral nerve, ensuring careful dissection to avoid nerve damage.
The surgeon exposes the inferior gluteal and internal pudendal arteries, revealing their anatomical positions and relationships within the surgical field.
The surgeon clips and cuts the internal pudendal artery, completing the vascular control in the surgical field.
The surgeon isolates the proximal side of the internal pudental artery, clips it, and then cuts the artery.
The surgeon carefully preserves the inferior gluteal artery, ensuring it remains intact during the procedure.
The surgeon removes the lymph nodes, including metastatic lymph nodes, as a single mass from the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully preserves the hypogastric nerve and pelvic plexus, ensuring these structures remain intact during the procedure.
The surgeon resects both the internal iliac artery and vein to facilitate the removal of metastatic lymph nodes, while preserving the superior vesicle vessels.
